summary:
The masseter muscle does not typically cause postural retraction but contributes more significantly to distraction of fragmented sections. In contrast, the mylohyoid and anterior bellies of the digastric are consistently identified across studies as causing posterior and infero-posterior movement—especially when both sides are affected—and thus play pivotal roles in displacing the mandible backward under trauma conditions. 

Final Answer: \boxed{B}

We are asked only to summarize the agent's *reasoning*, focusing strictly on clinically-relevant facts about which muscles pull the mandible back after bilateral mandibular fracture.

The agent first acknowledges earlier claims involving the masseter being influential in rearward motion but then refutes those based on direct textual evidence showing that the masseter mainly distracts bone ends apart—a destabilizing force opposed to realignment. More importantly, consistent documentation identifies the mylohyoid and anterior belly of the digastric musculature specifically as drivers behind posterior displacement, particularly in cases affecting both sides of the jawline. This leads to concluding that choice B best represents known physiological behavior related to mandibular segment relocation upon fracture.

Therefore, although some initial assumptions were made regarding powerful muscular contractions leading to retrusion, actual anatomical understanding reveals distinct functions per group—the masseters create tension away from each other whereas smaller soft-tissue structures near mid-line actively draw down and backwards through ligamentous connections at lower levels within oral cavity floor anatomy. These findings collectively point decisively towards identification of specific non-masseteric contributors whose activity results in measurable malpositioning observable clinically immediately post-injury.
